1→# Kapow — College Power Search: Product Specification
     2→
     3→## 1. Product Overview
     4→
     5→**Kapow (College Power Search)** is a web application that provides a comprehensive, faceted search engine for US colleges and universities. It aggregates data from 12 independent sources into a single searchable interface, enabling users to filter, sort, compare, and export institution data across 48 searchable dimensions.
     6→
     7→### Target Users
     8→
     9→- **Prospective students** researching colleges that fit their academic, financial, geographic, and lifestyle preferences
    10→- **Parents and families** comparing cost, outcomes, and value across institutions
    11→- **High school counselors** building filtered shortlists and exporting data for students
    12→- **AI assistants** querying college data programmatically via the MCP (Model Context Protocol) endpoint
    13→
    14→### Core Value Proposition
    15→
    16→College Power Search consolidates data that is otherwise scattered across dozens of websites — federal statistics, multiple ranking publications, athletic conferences, climate data, partisan lean scores, and application platform listings — into a single interface with real-time faceted filtering. Users can combine any number of criteria simultaneously, view results in four different formats, create custom ranking blends from multiple sources, and export their findings as CSV or PDF.
    17→
    18→---
    19→
    20→## 2. Feature Inventory
    21→
    22→### 2.1 Faceted Search Engine
    23→
    24→The search engine exposes 48 filterable fields organized into 11 active themes (with 7 additional theme slots reserved for future expansion). Every filter is applied server-side as a parameterized SQL WHERE clause against a denormalized materialized view (`dataset_merged`), ensuring fast queries across the full dataset.
    25→
    26→**Active themes and their fields:**
    27→
    28→| Theme | Fields | Description |
    29→|---|---|---|
    30→| Core | Institution Name (full-text), Unit ID | Name search across institution name, city, and state |
    31→| Location | City, State, Geographic Proximity, Locale, Region, Metro Area, Metro Distance | Where the school is and what kind of setting it occupies |
    32→| Academics | Institution Type, Highest Degree, Enrollment, Graduate Enrollment, Student-Faculty Ratio, Full-Time Faculty Rate, Gender (coed/single-sex), Online Only, Carnegie Classification | Academic profile and size |
    33→| Cost & Financial Aid | Avg Net Price, In-State Tuition, Out-of-State Tuition, Room & Board, Net Price (low-income), Pell Grant Rate, Federal Loan Rate, Endowment | What it costs and how it is funded |
    34→| Outcomes | Admission Rate, Graduation Rate, Retention Rate, Transfer Rate | Student success metrics |
    35→| Rankings | US News National, US News Liberal Arts, Forbes, Washington Monthly National, Washington Monthly Liberal Arts, Niche, Money Score | Rankings from 6 publications (7 lists) |
    36→| Admissions | Application Platforms (Common App, Coalition, Universal) | How to apply |
    37→| Athletics | NCAA Division, NCAA Conference | Athletic conference and competition level |
    38→| Fields of Study | CIP-coded program offerings | What majors and programs are available |
    39→| Cultural Environment | Partisan Direction, Partisan Score, Congressional District | Political leaning of the surrounding area |
    40→| Weather | Koppen Climate Group, Koppen Temperature Subtype, Koppen Precipitation Subtype | Climate classification of the campus location |
    41→
    42→**Filter types supported:**
    43→
    44→| Filter Type | Behavior | Example |
    45→|---|---|---|
    46→| Full-text search | PostgreSQL `tsvector` matching | "MIT" matches Massachusetts Institute of Technology |
    47→| Substring match | Case-insensitive partial match | "Arbor" matches Ann Arbor |
    48→| Single enum | Exact match from a fixed option list | Institution Type = "Public" |
    49→| Multi enum | Match any or all from a set | State in [CA, NY, MA] |
    50→| Numeric range | Min/max bounds on a numeric value | Tuition between $5,000 and $20,000 |
    51→| Exact string | Case-insensitive exact match | Metro Area = "Boston" |
    52→| Exact numeric | Exact integer match | Carnegie Classification = 15 |
    53→| Geographic proximity | PostGIS radius search | Within 50 miles of a given lat/lng |
    54→| Ranking | Numeric range on ranking position | US News rank 1-25 |
    55→
    56→### 2.2 Four Result Views
    57→
    58→Users can switch between four ways to view search results:
    59→
    60→| View | Description |
    61→|---|---|
    62→| **Card** (default) | Responsive grid of summary cards showing key stats per institution |
    63→| **List** | Compact row-based list emphasizing quick scanning |
    64→| **Table** | Full data table with sortable columns, customizable column visibility, and drag-to-reorder |
    65→| **Map** | Leaflet-based map with institution markers; supports click-to-detail |
    66→
    67→The selected view mode persists in `localStorage` and can also be set via URL parameter (`?view=table`).
    68→
    69→### 2.3 Custom Ranking Blends
    70→
    71→Users can create weighted composite rankings by assigning weights to any combination of the 7 ranking fields. When a blend is active, results are sorted by the computed blend score rather than by a single column. Blend weights are serialized in the URL (`?blend=...`) so blended searches can be shared via link.
    72→
    73→### 2.4 Sorting and Pagination
    74→
    75→- Any field marked as `sortable` can be used as the sort column (ascending or descending)
    76→- When a user location is set, results can be sorted by distance
    77→- Pagination supports configurable page sizes (5, 10, 20, 50, 100) with a maximum of 100 results per search
    78→- All sort and pagination state is encoded in URL parameters for shareability
    79→
    80→### 2.5 Column Customization (Table View)
    81→
    82→In table view, users can:
    83→- Show or hide any output field using a column picker
    84→- Reorder columns via drag-and-drop or up/down controls
    85→- Column visibility and order persist in `localStorage`
    86→- Default-visible columns are determined by field metadata from the API
    87→
    88→### 2.6 Institution Detail
    89→
    90→Clicking any institution in any view opens a detail drawer/modal showing expanded information including:
    91→- Core identity (name, city, state, coordinates)
    92→- Institution type and enrollment
    93→- Admission and graduation rates
    94→- Tuition (in-state and out-of-state) and average net price
    95→- Retention rate and student-faculty ratio
    96→
    97→### 2.7 Similar Institutions
    98→
    99→The API provides a `similarInstitutions` query that, given a specific institution, returns other schools with similar profiles based on type, enrollment, admission rate, graduation rate, and tuition. Results include a similarity distance score.
   100→
   101→### 2.8 CSV and PDF Export
   102→
   103→- **CSV Export**: Server-side export via `/api/export/csv` that respects active filters, sort order, and user location. Downloads directly as a file. Includes all exportable fields.
   104→- **PDF Export**: Client-side PDF generation using the current result set, active output fields, and active filter labels. Includes data source attribution.
   105→
   106→### 2.9 Geographic Features
   107→
   108→- **Proximity search**: PostGIS-powered `ST_DWithin` queries with configurable radius (up to ~310 miles / 500 km)
   109→- **Distance calculation**: When a user location is set, each result includes its distance from that location
   110→- **My Price**: When a user's home state is known, the system returns in-state tuition for public schools in that state and out-of-state tuition otherwise
   111→- **Map view**: Interactive Leaflet map with markers for all results in the current page
   112→- **ZIP code resolution**: The `resolveZip` query resolves a US ZIP code to latitude/longitude and state, used by the `LocationInput` component for location-by-ZIP entry (queries `location.zip_coordinates`)
   113→
   114→### 2.10 Authentication and User Preferences
   115→
   116→- **Authentication**: Supabase-based authentication supporting Google, GitHub, and email sign-in via OAuth
   117→- **User profile**: The `me` query returns the authenticated user's ID, email, display name, avatar, and auth provider
   118→- **Preferences**: Authenticated users can save a home location (latitude, longitude, state) that automatically populates the user location for distance calculations and My Price computation
   119→
   120→### 2.11 Sample Searches
   121→
   122→The home page features a carousel of pre-built sample searches that demonstrate the system's filtering capabilities:
   123→
   124→| Sample | Filters Applied |
   125→|---|---|
   126→| Top 25 National | US News National rank <= 25 |
   127→| Affordable in California | State = CA, net price <= $20,000 |
   128→| D1 Small Schools | NCAA Division I, enrollment <= 5,000 |
   129→| Selective & Affordable | Admission rate <= 30%, net price <= $20,000 |
   130→| D3 New England | NCAA Division III, New England region |
   131→| Sunny & Affordable | Dry (Koppen B) climate, net price <= $15,000 |
   132→
   133→Each sample search shows a live preview of matching institutions before the user navigates to the full search page.
   134→
   135→### 2.12 MCP (Model Context Protocol) Endpoint
   136→
   137→The application exposes a WebMCP interface that allows AI clients (such as Claude) to programmatically search the college database. Two tools are registered:
   138→
   139→| Tool | Description |
   140→|---|---|
   141→| `search_colleges` | Execute a filtered search with field/value pairs, limit, and offset |
   142→| `get_search_fields` | Retrieve the full field descriptor catalog including types and options |
   143→
   144→### 2.13 Data Source Attribution
   145→
   146→Every search field is linked to its originating data source. The UI can display source badges and tooltips, and the `dataSources` GraphQL query returns the full catalog with names, short names, URLs, and descriptions. This attribution carries through to PDF exports.
   147→
   148→---
   149→
   150→## 3. Pages
   151→
   152→| Route | Page | Description |
   153→|---|---|---|
   154→| `/` | Home | Landing page with hero search form (name, state, ranking, major), sample search carousel, and feature highlights |
   155→| `/search` | Search | Main search interface with collapsible filter sidebar (desktop: fixed 280px sidebar; mobile: toggle-to-show), results area with view mode toggle, export buttons, pagination, and institution detail drawer |
   156→| `/about` | About | Information page about the application |
   157→| `/auth/callback` | Auth Callback | OAuth redirect handler for Supabase authentication |
   158→
   159→All pages share a common `Header` (with navigation and auth controls) and `Footer`.
   160→
   161→---
   162→
   163→## 4. User Stories
   164→
   165→### Student
   166→
   167→- As a student, I want to search for colleges by name so I can quickly find a specific school.
   168→- As a student, I want to filter by state, cost, and admission rate simultaneously so I can find affordable schools I have a realistic chance of attending.
   169→- As a student, I want to filter by fields of study so I can find schools that offer my intended major.
   170→- As a student, I want to see how far each school is from my home so I can gauge travel time.
   171→- As a student, I want to view results on a map so I can see the geographic distribution of matching schools.
   172→- As a student, I want to create a custom ranking blend weighting cost and graduation rate so I can find the best value schools by my own criteria.
   173→
   174→### Parent
   175→
   176→- As a parent, I want to compare in-state and out-of-state tuition so I can understand the true cost.
   177→- As a parent, I want to see my personalized net price (My Price) so I know what public schools in my state would actually cost.
   178→- As a parent, I want to sort by graduation rate so I can prioritize schools where students succeed.
   179→- As a parent, I want to export the shortlist as a PDF so I can share it with my student.
   180→
   181→### Counselor
   182→
   183→- As a counselor, I want to export a filtered list as CSV so I can analyze it in a spreadsheet.
   184→- As a counselor, I want to use the table view with custom columns so I can compare specific metrics across many schools.
   185→- As a counselor, I want to share a search via URL so I can send a pre-filtered view to a student or family.
   186→- As a counselor, I want to use sample searches as starting points for common student profiles.
   187→
   188→### Authenticated User
   189→
   190→- As a returning user, I want to save my home location so distance and My Price are calculated automatically on every visit.
   191→- As a user, I want my column layout and view mode preferences to persist between sessions.
   192→
   193→### AI Client
   194→
   195→- As an AI assistant, I want to query available search fields so I can understand what filters exist.
   196→- As an AI assistant, I want to search colleges with structured filters so I can answer user questions about college options.
   197→
   198→---
   199→
   200→## 5. Data Sources
   201→
   202→| # | Source | Short Name | Data Provided | URL |
   203→|---|---|---|---|---|
   204→| 1 | National Center for Education Statistics (IPEDS) | NCES | Academics, cost, outcomes, enrollment, demographics, retention, graduation rates, faculty ratios, endowments | nces.ed.gov/ipeds |
   205→| 2 | U.S. News & World Report — National Universities | US News | National university rankings | usnews.com |
   206→| 3 | U.S. News & World Report — Liberal Arts Colleges | US News | Liberal arts college rankings | usnews.com |
   207→| 4 | Forbes America's Top Colleges | Forbes | Overall college rankings | forbes.com |
   208→| 5 | Washington Monthly College Rankings | Wash Monthly | Rankings emphasizing social mobility, research, and public service (national + liberal arts) | washingtonmonthly.com |
   209→| 6 | Niche Best Colleges | Niche | Rankings based on academics, value, and student reviews | niche.com |
   210→| 7 | Money Magazine Best Colleges | Money | College value scores balancing quality, affordability, and outcomes | money.com |
   211→| 8 | National Collegiate Athletic Association | NCAA | Division and conference data for intercollegiate athletics | ncaa.org |
   212→| 9 | FiveThirtyEight Partisan Lean | 538 | Congressional-district-level partisan lean direction and scores | fivethirtyeight.com |
   213→| 10 | Koppen Climate Classification | Koppen | Climate group, temperature subtype, and precipitation subtype | wikipedia.org (Koppen) |
   214→| 11 | College Application Platforms | Apps | Which platforms each school accepts (Common App, Coalition, Universal) | commonapp.org |
   215→| 12 | U.S. Census Bureau | Census | Metropolitan area names, distances, and IPEDS locale classifications | census.gov |
   216→
   217→---
   218→
   219→## 6. Architecture Overview
   220→
   221→Kapow is a monorepo with a Go API backend, a React frontend, and a PostgreSQL
   222→database. For detailed architecture documentation, see:
   223→
   224→- **[Frontend Architecture](frontend-architecture.md)** — tech stack, components, state management, GraphQL integration
   225→- **[GraphQL API](graphql-api.md)** — queries, mutations, types, request lifecycle
   226→- **[Search Engine](search-engine.md)** — field types, SQL generation, themes, data sources
   227→- **[Data Model](data-model.md)** — schemas, tables, relationships, migrations
   228→- **[Authentication](authentication.md)** — JWT flow, middleware, Supabase integration
   229→- **[Deployment](deployment.md)** — Docker images, Compose, env vars, startup sequence
   230→

1→# Search Engine Specification
     2→
     3→The Kapow search engine lives in `api/internal/search/`. It translates typed
     4→JSON filter payloads into parameterized SQL WHERE clauses executed against the
     5→`search_base.dataset_merged` materialized view. The engine is entirely
     6→stateless: no in-process caches, no background workers. Every request produces
     7→a fresh SQL query, and PostgreSQL indexes do the heavy lifting.
     8→
     9→---
    10→
    11→## Package Layout
    12→
    13→| File | Responsibility |
    14→|------|---------------|
    15→| `registry.go` | Field registry: `NewRegistry()`, `allFields()`, `Lookup()`, `Descriptors()`, `OutputDescriptors()`, `ExportableDescriptors()` |
    16→| `field.go` | `Field` interface, `baseField` embedding, concrete field type structs, `FieldDescriptor` metadata, `Theme` enum, `FieldVisibility`, `FieldSearchability` |
    17→| `kind.go` | `FacetKind` constants (`full_text`, `numeric_range`, `geo`, etc.) |
    18→| `source.go` | `FieldSource` struct, `SourceKind` constants, `SQLRef()` / `SQLRefAs()` helpers, JSONB value type mapping |
    19→| `filter.go` | Filter input structs deserialized from JSON (`FullTextFilter`, `NumericRangeFilter`, `GeoFilterInput`, etc.) |
    20→| `query.go` | `FilterInput`, `SearchRequest`, `SearchResult`, `SearchResponse` types; pagination constants |
    21→| `sql.go` | `BuildQuery()`, `BuildCountQuery()`, `BuildExportQuery()`, `BuildInstitutionQuery()`, `buildWhere()`, `buildOrderBy()` |
    22→| `validate.go` | `ValidateRequest()` -- collects all validation errors without short-circuiting |
    23→| `blend.go` | `BlendInput`, `BlendWeight`, `RankingProvider`, `buildBlendExpression()` |
    24→| `similarity.go` | `BuildSimilarInstitutionsQuery()` -- Euclidean distance similarity search |
    25→| `datasource.go` | `DataSource` struct and the catalog of 12 data sources |
    26→| `field_name_gen.go` | Generated field name constants |
    27→
    28→---
    29→
    30→## Core Abstractions
    31→
    32→### Field Interface
    33→
    34→Every search field implements the `Field` interface:
    35→
    36→```go
    37→type Field interface {
    38→    Name() string
    39→    Label() string
    40→    Description() string
    41→    Kind() FacetKind
    42→    Theme() Theme
    43→    Source() FieldSource
    44→    Descriptor() FieldDescriptor
    45→    BuildSQL(rawFilter json.RawMessage, argStart int) (expr string, args []any, err error)
    46→}
    47→```
    48→
    49→`BuildSQL` is the central method. It receives the raw JSON filter payload and
    50→the 1-based placeholder index, and returns a boolean SQL expression with
    51→corresponding bind arguments. The caller chains these expressions with AND.
    52→
    53→### FieldDescriptor
    54→
    55→`FieldDescriptor` is the JSON-serializable metadata contract consumed by both
    56→the GraphQL `searchFields` query and the REST metadata endpoint:
    57→
    58→| Property | Type | Purpose |
    59→|----------|------|---------|
    60→| `Name` | string | Filter key in API requests |
    61→| `Label` | string | Human-readable display name |
    62→| `Description` | string | Tooltip / help text |
    63→| `Kind` | FacetKind | Determines which filter UI to render |
    64→| `Theme` | Theme | UI grouping category |
    65→| `Options` | []EnumOption | Allowed values for enum fields |
    66→| `Bounds` | *NumericBounds | Hard min/max for numeric fields; max radius for geo fields |
    67→| `Units` | string | Display unit (USD, students, miles, rank) |
    68→| `SelectionMode` | SelectionMode | `any` or `all` for multi-enum |
    69→| `Visibility` | FieldVisibility | `VISIBLE`, `RESTRICTED`, or `HIDDEN` |
    70→| `Searchability` | FieldSearchability | `SEARCHABLE`, `RESTRICTED`, or `NOT_SEARCHABLE` |
    71→
    72→> **Note on RESTRICTED**: The `RESTRICTED` value is defined in both
    73→> `FieldVisibility` and `FieldSearchability` enums but is **not currently
    74→> enforced**. No fields use `RESTRICTED`, and no auth checks exist in
    75→> `ValidateRequest`, `BuildQuery`, or the field metadata resolvers to filter
    76→> fields by user role. It is a placeholder for future role-based field access.
    77→| `Exportable` | *bool | Whether the field can appear in CSV exports |
    78→| `OutputDefault` | *bool | `nil` = not an output field; `true` = shown by default; `false` = available in column picker |
    79→| `Sortable` | bool | Whether results can be sorted by this field |
    80→| `OutputKey` | string | Dot-path into the data JSONB blob for value extraction |
    81→| `DataSourceID` | string | References the originating data source |
    82→
    83→### baseField Embedding
    84→
    85→All concrete field types embed `baseField`, which provides default
    86→implementations of `Name()`, `Label()`, `Description()`, `Kind()`, `Theme()`,
    87→`Source()`, and `Descriptor()`. Fields that need extra descriptor properties
    88→(Options, Bounds, Units) override `Descriptor()` and extend the base result.
    89→
    90→---
    91→
    92→## Field Types
    93→
    94→### FullTextSearchField
    95→
    96→| Property | Value |
    97→|----------|-------|
    98→| FacetKind | `full_text` |
    99→| Filter struct | `FullTextFilter { Query string }` |
   100→| SQL pattern | `tsv_col @@ plainto_tsquery('english', $N)` |
   101→| Use case | Institution name search across name, city, and state |
   102→
   103→Uses PostgreSQL `plainto_tsquery` for natural-language tokenization. The source
   104→is a TSVector column (`name_location_search_vector`) maintained by the
   105→materialized view.
   106→
   107→### SubstringField
   108→
   109→| Property | Value |
   110→|----------|-------|
   111→| FacetKind | `substring` |
   112→| Filter struct | `SubstringFilter { Value string }` |
   113→| SQL pattern | `ref ILIKE '%' \|\| $N \|\| '%'` |
   114→| Use case | Partial text matching on city, conference name |
   115→
   116→For JSONB sources, a NULL guard is prepended: `ref IS NOT NULL AND ...`.
   117→
   118→### ExactStringField
   119→
   120→| Property | Value |
   121→|----------|-------|
   122→| FacetKind | `exact_string` |
   123→| Filter struct | `ExactStringFilter { Value string }` |
   124→| SQL pattern | `ref = $N` (case-sensitive) or `ref ILIKE $N` (case-insensitive) |
   125→| Use case | Unit ID lookup, metro area name match |
   126→
   127→The `CaseSensitive` flag on the struct controls the operator choice.
   128→
   129→### ExactNumericField
   130→
   131→| Property | Value |
   132→|----------|-------|
   133→| FacetKind | `exact_numeric` |
   134→| Filter struct | `ExactNumericFilter { Value float64 }` |
   135→| SQL pattern | `(ref)::integer = $N` (JSONB) or `col = $N` (column) |
   136→| Use case | Carnegie classification code, congressional district |
   137→
   138→For JSONB sources the extracted text is cast to the field's declared value type.
   139→
   140→### NumericRangeField
   141→
   142→| Property | Value |
   143→|----------|-------|
   144→| FacetKind | `numeric_range` |
   145→| Filter struct | `NumericRangeFilter { Min *float64, Max *float64 }` |
   146→| SQL pattern | `ref BETWEEN $N AND $M`, `ref >= $N`, or `ref <= $N` |
   147→| Use case | Tuition, enrollment, acceptance rate, scores |
   148→
   149→At least one of Min or Max must be provided. Optional `HardMin` / `HardMax`
   150→bounds are enforced during validation. The field carries `Units` (USD,
   151→students, etc.) for display.
   152→
   153→### RankingField
   154→
   155→| Property | Value |
   156→|----------|-------|
   157→| FacetKind | `numeric_range` |
   158→| Filter struct | `NumericRangeFilter` (same as NumericRangeField) |
   159→| SQL pattern | `(data #>> '{key,method}') = 'rank' AND (ref) IS NOT NULL AND (ref)::integer BETWEEN $N AND $M` |
   160→| Use case | US News, Forbes, Washington Monthly, Niche ranking ranges |
   161→
   162→Embeds `NumericRangeField` but overrides `BuildSQL` to add a method guard.
   163→Only rows where the ranking dataset's `method` key equals `'rank'` are
   164→included, preventing score-based entries from matching rank-based filters.
   165→
   166→### GeoField
   167→
   168→| Property | Value |
   169→|----------|-------|
   170→| FacetKind | `geo` |
   171→| Filter struct | `GeoFilterInput { Kind, Radius?, BBox? }` |
   172→| SQL patterns | Radius: `ST_DWithin(col, ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint($lng, $lat), 4326), $km * 1000)` |
   173→| | BBox: `ST_Intersects(col, ST_MakeEnvelope($minLng, $minLat, $maxLng, $maxLat, 4326))` |
   174→| Use case | "Colleges within 50 km of Boston" |
   175→
   176→Supports two sub-kinds via a discriminated union. The `MaxRadiusKm` field
   177→(currently 500 km) is enforced during validation. Coordinates use SRID 4326.
   178→Radius distances are in metres internally (km * 1000).
   179→
   180→### SingleEnumField
   181→
   182→| Property | Value |
   183→|----------|-------|
   184→| FacetKind | `single_enum` |
   185→| Filter struct | `SingleEnumFilter { Value string }` |
   186→| SQL pattern | `ref = $N` |
   187→| Use case | State, region, institution type, NCAA division, climate |
   188→
   189→Carries a fixed `Options` slice for validation and UI rendering. Validation
   190→rejects values not in the options list.
   191→
   192→### GenderField
   193→
   194→| Property | Value |
   195→|----------|-------|
   196→| FacetKind | `single_enum` (virtual) |
   197→| Filter struct | `SingleEnumFilter { Value string }` |
   198→| SQL pattern | Varies by value: `men-only`, `women-only`, or `coed` |
   199→| Use case | Filter by student body gender composition |
   200→
   201→A virtual enum backed by two JSONB boolean keys (`data #>> '{nces,men_only}'`
   202→and `data #>> '{nces,women_only}'`). The `coed` option matches rows where both
   203→flags are 0 or NULL. This avoids schema changes while providing a clean filter
   204→interface.
   205→
   206→### MultiEnumField
   207→
   208→| Property | Value |
   209→|----------|-------|
   210→| FacetKind | `multi_enum` |
   211→| Filter struct | `MultiEnumFilter { Values []string }` |
   212→| SQL patterns | Array/any: `ref ?| $N::text[]` |
   213→| | Array/all: `ref @> $N::jsonb` |
   214→| | Object-key/any: `ref ?| $N::text[]` |
   215→| | Object-key/all: `ref ?& $N::text[]` |
   216→| Use case | Application platforms, fields of study |
   217→
   218→Supports two storage formats (`array`, `object_key`) and two selection modes
   219→(`any`, `all`), yielding four SQL generation paths. The storage format reflects
   220→how the data is stored in the JSONB blob.
   221→
   222→### MultiColumnEnumField
   223→
   224→| Property | Value |
   225→|----------|-------|
   226→| FacetKind | `multi_enum` |
   227→| Filter struct | `MultiEnumFilter { Values []string }` |
   228→| SQL pattern | `col = ANY($N::text[])` |
   229→| Use case | State multi-select |
   230→
   231→Unlike `MultiEnumField` which operates on JSONB, this field targets a direct
   232→column. The filter matches rows where the column value is any one of the
   233→provided choices.
   234→
   235→---
   236→
   237→## Field Sources
   238→
   239→`FieldSource` describes where in `search_base.dataset_merged` a field's value
   240→lives.
   241→
   242→| SourceKind | SQLRef() Output | Description |
   243→|------------|----------------|-------------|
   244→| `column` | `column_name` | Direct top-level column (e.g., `unit_id`, `city`, `state`) |
   245→| `tsvector` | `column_name` | PostgreSQL TSVector column for full-text search |
   246→| `jsonb_path` | `data #>> '{k1,k2,...}'` | Path into the `data` JSONB column; extracts as text |
   247→| `geo` | `column_name` | PostGIS geography column (`coordinates`) |
   248→
   249→For JSONB paths, `SQLRefAs(castType)` wraps the reference in a PostgreSQL
   250→cast: `(data #>> '{k1,k2}')::integer`. The `JSONBValueType` enum
   251→(`text`, `numeric`, `integer`, `boolean`, `array`, `object`) determines the
   252→appropriate cast.

   305→---
   306→
   307→## SQL Generation
   308→
   309→### Query Pipeline
   310→
   311→1. **Validation** -- `ValidateRequest()` checks all filters, pagination bounds,
   312→   sort field validity, geo radius limits, enum option membership, blend
   313→   weights, and user location coordinates. All errors are collected (no
   314→   short-circuit).
   315→
   316→2. **WHERE clause assembly** -- `buildWhere()` iterates over `FilterInput`
   317→   items, looks up each field in the registry, calls `BuildSQL()`, and
   318→   concatenates the resulting boolean expressions with AND. Placeholder indices
   319→   (`$1`, `$2`, ...) are advanced globally across all filters.
   320→
   321→3. **SELECT construction** -- `BuildQuery()` assembles the full SELECT
   322→   statement with 10 columns: `unit_id`, `institution_name`, `city`, `state`,
   323→   `latitude`, `longitude`, distance expression, myPrice expression, `data`
   324→   (JSONB), and blend score.
   325→
   326→4. **Computed columns**:
   327→   - **Distance**: `ST_Distance(coordinates, ST_SetSRID(ST_MakePoint($lng, $lat), 4326)::geography) / 1609.344`
   328→     (metres to miles). Only present when `UserLocation` is provided.
   329→   - **MyPrice**: `CASE WHEN state = $state THEN in_state_tuition ELSE out_of_state_tuition END`.
   330→     Only present when `UserLocation.State` is non-empty.
   331→   - **BlendScore**: Weighted normalized average across ranking fields (see
   332→     Ranking Blend below). Only present when `Blend` is provided.
   333→
   334→5. **ORDER BY** -- When a blend is active, results are ordered by blend score
   335→   ascending (lower is better). Otherwise, `buildOrderBy()` handles built-in
   336→   sorts (`distance` by column position 7, `name` by `institution_name`) and
   337→   registry field sorts (column or JSONB-cast reference). NULL handling:
   338→   `NULLS LAST` for ASC, `NULLS FIRST` for DESC.
   339→
   340→6. **Pagination** -- `LIMIT $N OFFSET $M` with defaults (limit=20, max=100).
   341→   The total offset+limit is capped at `MaxLimit` (100) to prevent deep
   342→   pagination.
   343→
   344→### Query Variants
   345→
   346→| Function | Purpose | Differences from BuildQuery |
   347→|----------|---------|---------------------------|
   348→| `BuildQuery()` | Primary search | Full pagination (limit + offset) |
   349→| `BuildCountQuery()` | Total result count | `SELECT COUNT(*)`, no ORDER BY / LIMIT / OFFSET |
   350→| `BuildExportQuery()` | CSV export | No offset; limit capped by `maxRows` parameter |
   351→| `BuildInstitutionQuery()` | Single institution detail | Hardcoded columns; `WHERE unit_id = $1` |
   352→
   353→### Parameterization
   354→
   355→All user-supplied values flow through `$N` placeholders. Field values, sort
   356→parameters, pagination limits, user coordinates, and state codes are all bound
   357→as query arguments. SQL expressions use `fmt.Sprintf` only for column
   358→references and structural SQL -- never for user data.
   359→
   360→---
   361→
   362→## Ranking Blend
   363→
   364→The blend system computes a weighted composite score across multiple ranking
   365→providers. Seven providers are eligible:
   366→
   367→| Field Name | Max Value | Direction |
   368→|------------|-----------|-----------|
   369→| `rank_us_news_national` | 440 | Lower is better |
   370→| `rank_us_news_liberal_arts` | 230 | Lower is better |
   371→| `rank_forbes` | 500 | Lower is better |
   372→| `rank_washington_monthly_national` | 400 | Lower is better |
   373→| `rank_washington_monthly_liberal_arts` | 250 | Lower is better |
   374→| `rank_niche` | 1600 | Lower is better |
   375→| `score_money` | 5.0 | Higher is better |
   376→
   377→### Normalization
   378→
   379→Each ranking value is normalized to [0, 1] where 0 is best:
   380→
   381→- **Lower-is-better (ranks)**: `COALESCE((ref)::numeric / maxVal, 1.0)`
   382→- **Higher-is-better (scores)**: `COALESCE(1.0 - (ref)::numeric / maxVal, 1.0)`
   383→
   384→NULL values normalize to 1.0 (worst), penalizing unranked institutions.
   385→
   386→### Composite Expression
   387→
   388→```sql
   389→(w1 * norm1 + w2 * norm2 + ...) / (w1 + w2 + ...)
   390→```
   391→
   392→The expression uses only constants (no parameterized args) and is embedded
   393→directly in the SELECT list. When a blend is active, results are ordered by
   394→`blend_score ASC NULLS LAST`.
   395→
   396→---
   397→
   398→## Similar Institutions
   399→
   400→`BuildSimilarInstitutionsQuery()` finds institutions most similar to a given
   401→one using Euclidean distance across normalized dimensions:
   402→
   403→- **Min-max normalized**: enrollment (log-scale), tuition (in-state and
   404→  out-of-state), ACT midpoint, SAT math midpoint
   405→- **Rate dimensions** (already 0-1): graduation rate, retention rate,
   406→  admission rate
   407→- **Categorical penalty**: +0.09 when institution type (control) differs
   408→
   409→The query uses a CTE to compute dataset-wide min/max statistics, retrieves the
   410→source institution's values, then computes `|/(sum of squared differences)` as
   411→the Euclidean distance. Results are ordered by distance ascending with a
   412→configurable limit (default 10, max 50).
   413→
   414→---
   415→
   416→## Validation
   417→
   418→`ValidateRequest()` performs comprehensive input validation:
   419→
   420→| Check | Sentinel Field | Rule |
   421→|-------|---------------|------|
   422→| Unknown field name | field name | Must exist in registry |
   423→| Filter JSON structure | field name | Must deserialize to the field's expected filter struct |
   424→| Text length | field name | Full-text, substring, exact-string queries capped at 512 characters |
   425→| Numeric range | field name | At least one of min/max required; values within hard bounds |
   426→| Enum membership | field name | Value must appear in field's Options (when Options is non-nil) |
   427→| Multi-enum count | field name | At least one value required |
   428→| Geo radius | field name | Must be positive; must not exceed `MaxRadiusKm` (500 km) |
   429→| Geo kind | field name | Must be `radius` or `bbox` |
   430→| Pagination limit | `_limit` | Must not exceed `MaxLimit` (100) |
   431→| Pagination offset | `_offset` | Must be non-negative |
   432→| User location | `_userLocation` | Latitude in [-90, 90]; longitude in [-180, 180]; state is 2-letter uppercase |
   433→| Blend weights | `_blend` | Field must be a known ranking provider; weight in [0, 100]; at least one non-zero |
   434→| Sort field | `_sort` | Must be `distance`, `name`, or a sortable registry field; distance requires userLocation |
   435→
   436→All errors are collected into a `[]ValidationError` slice. Validation never
   437→short-circuits -- callers receive every problem at once.
   438→
   439→---
   440→
   441→## Registry
   442→
   443→`NewRegistry()` populates a `map[string]Field` from the `allFields()` function
   444→in `registry.go`. The registry provides:
   445→
   446→| Method | Returns | Purpose |
   447→|--------|---------|---------|
   448→| `Lookup(name)` | `(Field, bool)` | O(1) field lookup by name |
   449→| `All()` | `[]Field` | All registered fields (unordered) |
   450→| `Descriptors()` | `[]FieldDescriptor` | Metadata for every field |
   451→| `OutputDescriptors()` | `[]FieldDescriptor` | Fields with `OutputDefault != nil` (result columns) |
   452→| `ExportableDescriptors()` | `[]FieldDescriptor` | Exportable output fields, ordered by theme then registration order |
   453→
   454→---
   455→
   456→## Adding a New Field
   457→
   458→1. Add a field struct literal to the `allFields()` slice in `registry.go`.
   459→   Every field must explicitly set `visibility`, `searchability`, and
   460→   `exportable` -- there are no defaults.
   461→
   462→2. The field's `Name()` becomes the filter key in API requests and the
   463→   metadata endpoint.
   464→
   465→3. Choose the appropriate concrete type (`NumericRangeField`,
   466→   `SingleEnumField`, etc.) or create a new one if no existing type fits.
   467→
   468→4. If creating a new field type, implement the `Field` interface including
   469→   `BuildSQL()` in `field.go`.
   470→
   471→5. New fields are automatically covered by the table-driven
   472→   `TestBuildSQLAllFields` test in `search_test.go`. Add targeted test cases
   473→   for edge cases or new field types.
   474→
   475→6. Run `make api-test` to verify. For JSONB-backed fields, also run
   476→   integration tests against a real database to catch type encoding issues.
   477→

1→# Authentication Specification
     2→
     3→This document describes the authentication and authorization architecture for
     4→the Kapow API, covering JWT validation, claims structure, middleware chain,
     5→Supabase integration, user provisioning, and the frontend auth flow.
     6→
     7→Related specs: [Frontend Architecture](frontend-architecture.md),
     8→[Deployment](deployment.md).
     9→
    10→---
    11→
    12→## Overview
    13→
    14→Kapow uses JSON Web Tokens (JWT) for stateless authentication. Tokens are issued
    15→by Supabase Auth and validated by the API on every request to protected routes.
    16→Two validation strategies are supported: HMAC (HS256) for development and shared-
    17→secret setups, and RSA (RS256) for production environments using a public key.
    18→
    19→---
    20→
    21→## JWT Validation
    22→
    23→### Validator Interface
    24→
    25→The `auth.Validator` interface defines the single validation contract:
    26→
    27→```go
    28→type Validator interface {
    29→    Validate(tokenString string) (*Claims, error)
    30→}
    31→```
    32→
    33→All middleware depends on this interface, allowing transparent swapping between
    34→validator implementations.
    35→
    36→### HMACValidator (HS256)
    37→
    38→- **Implementation**: `internal/auth.HMACValidator`
    39→- **Config**: `JWT_SECRET` environment variable
    40→- **Algorithm**: HS256 (HMAC-SHA256 symmetric signing)
    41→- **Use case**: Local development, simple deployments, Supabase JWT secret
    42→
    43→Construction:
    44→
    45→```go
    46→validator := auth.NewHMACValidator(cfg.JWTSecret, parserOpts...)
    47→```
    48→
    49→The validator rejects tokens signed with any algorithm other than HMAC. If the
    50→signing method does not match `*jwt.SigningMethodHMAC`, validation fails with
    51→`auth: unexpected signing method`.
    52→
    53→### RSAValidator (RS256)
    54→
    55→- **Implementation**: `internal/auth.RSAValidator`
    56→- **Config**: `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` environment variable (PEM-encoded RSA public key)
    57→- **Algorithm**: RS256 (RSA-SHA256 asymmetric signing)
    58→- **Use case**: Production with key rotation, multi-service token sharing
    59→
    60→Construction:
    61→
    62→```go
    63→validator, err := auth.NewRSAValidator(cfg.JWTPublicKey, parserOpts...)
    64→```
    65→
    66→The PEM key is parsed at startup. If parsing fails, the API exits immediately.
    67→The validator rejects tokens signed with any algorithm other than RSA.
    68→
    69→### Validator Selection
    70→
    71→`JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` takes precedence when both `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` and `JWT_SECRET` are
    72→set. The decision is made once at startup in `cmd/server/main.go`:
    73→
    74→```
    75→if JWT_PUBLIC_KEY is set:
    76→    use RSAValidator(JWT_PUBLIC_KEY)
    77→else:
    78→    use HMACValidator(JWT_SECRET)
    79→```
    80→
    81→### Optional Issuer and Audience Validation
    82→
    83→Both validators accept `jwt.ParserOption` values for additional claim validation:
    84→
    85→| Config Variable | Parser Option | Effect |
    86→|-----------------|---------------|--------|
    87→| `JWT_ISSUER` | `jwt.WithIssuer(iss)` | Rejects tokens with non-matching `iss` claim |
    88→| `JWT_AUDIENCE` | `jwt.WithAudience(aud)` | Rejects tokens with non-matching `aud` claim |
    89→
    90→These are optional. When unset, issuer and audience are not validated. For
    91→Supabase, typical values are:
    92→
    93→```env
    94→JWT_ISSUER=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1
    95→JWT_AUDIENCE=authenticated
    96→```
    97→
    98→### Error Responses
    99→
   100→All validation failures return the same structure to avoid leaking implementation
   101→details:
   102→
   103→| Condition | Error message |
   104→|-----------|---------------|
   105→| Missing/malformed Authorization header | `missing or malformed authorization header` |
   106→| Expired token | `invalid or expired token` |
   107→| Wrong signing algorithm | `invalid or expired token` |
   108→| Signature mismatch | `invalid or expired token` |
   109→| Issuer/audience mismatch | `invalid or expired token` |
   110→
   111→---
   112→
   113→## Claims Structure
   114→
   115→```go
   116→type Claims struct {
   117→    jwt.RegisteredClaims                          // sub, iss, aud, exp, iat, nbf, jti
   118→    Email        string         `json:"email,omitempty"`
   119→    Role         string         `json:"role,omitempty"`           // Supabase singular role
   120→    Roles        []string       `json:"roles,omitempty"`          // Multi-role array
   121→    TeamID       string         `json:"team_id,omitempty"`
   122→    ClientType   string         `json:"client_type,omitempty"`    // web | cli | mobile | desktop
   123→    AppMetadata  map[string]any `json:"app_metadata,omitempty"`   // Provider info from Supabase
   124→    UserMetadata map[string]any `json:"user_metadata,omitempty"`  // Profile: full_name, avatar_url
   125→}
   126→```
   127→
   128→### Helper Methods
   129→
   130→| Method | Returns | Purpose |
   131→|--------|---------|---------|
   132→| `NormalizedRoles()` | `[]string` | Deduplicated union of `Roles` array and singular `Role` field |
   133→| `Provider()` | `string` | Extracts `app_metadata.provider` (e.g. `"google"`, `"github"`) |
   134→
   135→### Context Storage
   136→
   137→Claims are stored in and retrieved from `context.Context`:
   138→
   139→| Function | Behavior |
   140→|----------|----------|
   141→| `auth.SetClaims(ctx, claims)` | Stores `*Claims` in context |
   142→| `auth.GetClaims(ctx)` | Returns `*Claims` or `nil` if absent |
   143→| `auth.MustGetClaims(ctx)` | Returns `*Claims`; panics if absent (use only behind auth middleware) |
   144→
   145→---
   146→
   147→## Supabase JWT Claims Mapping
   148→
   149→Supabase Auth issues JWTs with the following structure. The API maps these to
   150→the `Claims` struct:
   151→
   152→| JWT Claim | Go Field | Type | Purpose |
   153→|-----------|----------|------|---------|
   154→| `sub` | `Claims.Subject` | `string` | Supabase user UUID -- used as `user_id` in DB |
   155→| `email` | `Claims.Email` | `string` | User email -- used as personal team name |
   156→| `role` | `Claims.Role` | `string` | `"authenticated"` or `"anon"` |
   157→| `iss` | `Claims.Issuer` | `string` | `https://<project>.supabase.co/auth/v1` |
   158→| `aud` | `Claims.Audience` | `[]string` | `["authenticated"]` |
   159→| `exp` | `Claims.ExpiresAt` | `*jwt.NumericDate` | Token expiry (Supabase default: 1 hour) |
   160→| `iat` | `Claims.IssuedAt` | `*jwt.NumericDate` | Token issue time |
   161→| `app_metadata.provider` | `Claims.AppMetadata` | `map[string]any` | Auth provider (`google`, `github`, `email`) |
   162→| `user_metadata.full_name` | `Claims.UserMetadata` | `map[string]any` | User display name |
   163→| `user_metadata.avatar_url` | `Claims.UserMetadata` | `map[string]any` | Profile image URL |
   164→
   165→### Example Supabase JWT Payload
   166→
   167→```json
   168→{
   169→  "sub": "a1b2c3d4-e5f6-7890-abcd-ef1234567890",
   170→  "email": "user@example.com",
   171→  "role": "authenticated",
   172→  "iss": "https://xyzproject.supabase.co/auth/v1",
   173→  "aud": "authenticated",
   174→  "exp": 1700000000,
   175→  "iat": 1699996400,
   176→  "app_metadata": {
   177→    "provider": "google",
   178→    "providers": ["google"]
   179→  },
   180→  "user_metadata": {
   181→    "full_name": "Jane Doe",
   182→    "avatar_url": "https://lh3.googleusercontent.com/..."
   183→  }
   184→}
   185→```
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→## Middleware Chain
   190→
   191→All middleware is defined in `api/internal/middleware/`.
   192→
   193→### Auth (Mandatory Authentication)
   194→
   195→**Function**: `middleware.Auth(validator auth.Validator) func(http.Handler) http.Handler`
   196→
   197→Behavior:
   198→1. Extracts the `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header
   199→2. If missing or malformed: returns **401** with `WWW-Authenticate` header
   200→3. Validates the token via the `Validator` interface
   201→4. If invalid: returns **401** with `WWW-Authenticate` header including `error="invalid_token"`
   202→5. If valid: stores `*auth.Claims` in context via `auth.SetClaims`, calls next handler
   203→
   204→The `WWW-Authenticate` header follows RFC 9728 and includes a reference to the
   205→OAuth 2.1 Protected Resource Metadata endpoint:
   206→
   207→```
   208→WWW-Authenticate: Bearer realm="kapow", resource_metadata="/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource"
   209→```
   210→
   211→On invalid token:
   212→
   213→```
   214→WWW-Authenticate: Bearer realm="kapow", error="invalid_token", resource_metadata="/.well-known/oauth-protected-resource"
   215→```
   216→
   217→### OptionalAuth (Anonymous Allowed)
   218→
   219→**Function**: `middleware.OptionalAuth(validator auth.Validator) func(http.Handler) http.Handler`
   220→
   221→Behavior:
   222→1. If no `Authorization` header: passes request through without claims (anonymous)
   223→2. If token present and valid: stores claims in context, calls next handler
   224→3. If token present and **invalid**: returns **401** (prevents use of bad tokens)
   225→
   226→This middleware allows both authenticated and anonymous access to an endpoint
   227→while still rejecting malformed or expired tokens.
   228→
   229→### EnsureUser (User Provisioning)
   230→
   231→**Function**: `middleware.EnsureUser(svc *user.Service) func(http.Handler) http.Handler`
   232→
   233→Must run after Auth or OptionalAuth middleware.
   234→
   235→Behavior:
   236→1. Reads `*auth.Claims` from context via `auth.GetClaims`
   237→2. If claims are `nil` or `Subject` is empty: passes through (anonymous request)
   238→3. Calls `user.Service.EnsureUser(ctx, subject, email)` to provision the user
   239→4. If provisioning fails: returns **500 Internal Server Error**
   240→5. If provisioning succeeds: calls next handler
   241→
   242→### Global Middleware Stack
   243→
   244→Applied to every request in order:
   245→
   246→1. `RequestID` -- generates or propagates `X-Request-ID`
   247→2. `Logging` -- structured slog request/response logging
   248→3. `Recovery` -- panic recovery with 500 response
   249→4. `HTTPDetail` -- optional HTTP debug header/body logging
   250→5. `RealIP` (chi) -- resolves client IP from proxy headers
   251→6. `StripSlashes` (chi) -- normalizes trailing slashes
   252→
   253→---
   254→
   255→## Protected Routes
   256→
   257→| Route | Middleware | Auth Behavior |
   258→|-------|-----------|---------------|
   259→| `POST /graphql` | OptionalAuth + EnsureUser | Anonymous allowed; user provisioned if token present |
   260→| `GET /graphql` | OptionalAuth + EnsureUser | Same as POST |
   261→| `/mcp/*` | Auth (mandatory) | 401 with `WWW-Authenticate` if unauthenticated; per MCP OAuth 2.1 spec |
   262→| `GET /api/export/csv` | OptionalAuth + EnsureUser | Anonymous allowed; user provisioned if token present |
   263→| `GET /private/healthcheck` | None | Internal health endpoint |
   264→| `GET /private/log-config` | None | Log configuration (read) |
   265→| `PUT /private/log-config` | None | Log configuration (update) |
   266→| `GET /playground` | None | GraphQL Playground (dev only, gated by `GRAPHQL_PLAYGROUND` config) |
   267→| `GET /.well-known/oauth-protected-resource` | None | OAuth 2.1 metadata (RFC 9728) |
   268→
   269→---
   270→
   271→## Frontend Auth Flow
   272→
   273→### Provider Setup
   274→
   275→The Supabase JS client is initialized in `web/src/lib/supabase.ts` using:
   276→
   277→- `VITE_SUPABASE_URL` -- Supabase project URL
   278→- `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` -- Supabase anonymous/public key
   279→
   280→Both are compile-time Vite environment variables (browser-safe).
   281→
   282→### Sign-In Flow
   283→
   284→1. User clicks "Sign In" in the `SignInMenu` component
   285→2. Supabase SDK initiates the OAuth flow (Google, GitHub, or email/password)
   286→3. Provider redirects back to `/auth/callback`
   287→4. `AuthCallback` page handles the redirect; Supabase SDK exchanges the code for tokens
   288→5. `authStore.initialize()` picks up the new session via `supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange`
   289→6. JWT is stored in the Supabase SDK session (in-memory + localStorage)
   290→
   291→### Token Attachment
   292→
   293→The urql GraphQL client uses `@urql/exchange-auth` to attach tokens:
   294→
   295→1. `addAuthToOperation`: reads the current token from `authStore.getToken()` (which returns `session.access_token`)
   296→2. Appends `Authorization: Bearer <token>` header to every GraphQL operation
   297→3. If no token is available (anonymous), the request proceeds without the header
   298→
   299→### Token Refresh
   300→
   301→1. `didAuthError`: triggers on 401 responses or GraphQL errors with code `FORBIDDEN` or `UNAUTHENTICATED`
   302→2. `refreshAuth`: calls `supabase.auth.getSession()` to get a fresh session/token
   303→3. Updates `authStore` with the new session
   304→4. urql automatically retries the failed operation with the new token
   305→
   306→### Session State
   307→
   308→The `authStore` (Zustand) manages session state:
   309→
   310→| Field | Type | Description |
   311→|-------|------|-------------|
   312→| `session` | `Session \| null` | Current Supabase session (includes tokens) |
   313→| `user` | `User \| null` | Supabase user object |
   314→| `loading` | `boolean` | True during initialization |
   315→
   316→The store subscribes to `supabase.auth.onAuthStateChange` for real-time session
   317→updates (login, logout, token refresh).
   318→
   319→### Sign-Out Flow
   320→
   321→1. User clicks "Sign Out" in the `UserMenu` component
   322→2. `authStore.signOut()` calls `supabase.auth.signOut()`
   323→3. Session and user are cleared from the store
   324→4. `preferencesStore.clear()` resets user preferences
   325→5. Subsequent requests proceed anonymously (no `Authorization` header)
   326→
   327→---
   328→
   329→## User Provisioning
   330→
   331→### Database Schema
   332→
   333→User data lives in the `subscription` schema:
   334→
   335→| Table | Key Columns | Purpose |
   336→|-------|-------------|---------|
   337→| `subscription.team` | `id` (varchar PK), `name` | Personal team (one per user) |
   338→| `subscription.team_member` | `id` (PK), `team_id` (FK), `user_id` (unique), `role` | Links user to team |
   339→
   340→### Provisioning Logic
   341→
   342→`user.Service.EnsureUser(ctx, userID, email)` runs a single SQL statement:
   343→
   344→1. **Check**: Does a `team_member` row exist for this `user_id`?
   345→2. **If not**: Insert a new `team` with `name = email`, then insert a `team_member`
   346→   with `role = OWNER` linking the user to the new team
   347→3. **If yes**: No-op (`ON CONFLICT (user_id) DO NOTHING`)
   348→
   349→The operation is idempotent and safe under concurrent requests. The CTE pattern
   350→ensures atomicity -- either both rows are created or neither is.
   351→
   352→### When Provisioning Runs
   353→
   354→Provisioning runs on every authenticated request via the `EnsureUser` middleware.
   355→For anonymous requests (no claims in context), the middleware is a no-op.
   356→
   357→---
   358→
   359→## Configuration Reference
   360→
   361→| Variable | Required | Default | Description |
   362→|----------|----------|---------|-------------|
   363→| `JWT_SECRET` | No* | (empty) | HS256 shared secret for HMAC validation |
   364→| `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` | No* | (empty) | PEM-encoded RSA public key for RS256 validation |
   365→| `JWT_ISSUER` | No | (empty) | Expected `iss` claim; skipped if unset |
   366→| `JWT_AUDIENCE` | No | (empty) | Expected `aud` claim; skipped if unset |
   367→| `OIDC_DISCOVERY_URL` | No | (empty) | Reserved for future OIDC auto-discovery |
   368→| `VITE_SUPABASE_URL` | No | (empty) | Supabase project URL (frontend) |
   369→| `VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY` | No | (empty) | Supabase public anon key (frontend) |
   370→
   371→*At least one of `JWT_SECRET` or `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` should be set for auth to work.
   372→If neither is set, the HMAC validator runs with an empty secret (all tokens fail
   373→validation).
   374→
   375→---
   376→
   377→## Security Considerations
   378→
   379→1. **Token exposure**: JWTs are transmitted as Bearer tokens in the `Authorization`
   380→   header. HTTPS is required in production to prevent interception.
   381→2. **Secret management**: `JWT_SECRET` must never be committed to version control.
   382→   Use `.env` (gitignored) or a secrets manager.
   383→3. **Token lifetime**: Supabase tokens default to 1-hour expiry. The frontend
   384→   handles automatic refresh via the auth exchange.
   385→4. **Algorithm confusion**: Both validators explicitly check the signing method
   386→   before returning the key, preventing algorithm-switching attacks.
   387→5. **Error uniformity**: Invalid tokens produce the same error message regardless
   388→   of failure reason, preventing information disclosure.
   389→6. **WWW-Authenticate**: All 401 responses include a `WWW-Authenticate` header
   390→   with a pointer to the OAuth 2.1 Protected Resource Metadata endpoint, enabling
   391→   MCP and other OAuth-aware clients to discover the authorization server.
   392→

content.text:
Excellent! I now have a complete picture of Kapow's product promises. Let me compile this into a comprehensive summary.

---

## KAPOW PRODUCT PROMISES TO USERS

Based on thorough review of `docs/specs/product-overview.md`, the home page (`Home.tsx`), About page (`About.tsx`), and GraphQL API schema, here are ALL user-facing promises organized by category:

### **1. SEARCH CAPABILITIES**

**Core Promise**: Access to a faceted search engine with 48 filterable fields across 11 active themes.

**Specific Claims**:
- "Find your perfect college — by academics, cost, location, athletics, and more"
- Search by **institutional profile**: name, unit ID, institution type, enrollment, highest degree, gender composition (coed/single-sex), online-only status, Carnegie classification
- Search by **location**: city, state, geographic proximity (configurable radius up to 500 km), locale classification, region, metro area, metro distance
- Search by **academics**: student-faculty ratio, full-time faculty rate
- Search by **cost & financial aid**: average net price, in-state tuition, out-of-state tuition, room & board, net price for low-income students, Pell grant rate, federal loan rate, endowment
- Search by **outcomes**: admission rate, graduation rate, retention rate, transfer rate
- Search by **rankings**: US News (national & liberal arts), Forbes, Washington Monthly (national & liberal arts), Niche, Money Magazine
- Search by **admissions**: application platform acceptance (Common App, Coalition, Universal)
- Search by **athletics**: NCAA division and conference membership
- Search by **fields of study**: CIP-coded program offerings
- Search by **cultural environment**: partisan direction/score, congressional district
- Search by **weather/climate**: Köppen climate classification (climate group, temperature subtype, precipitation subtype)
- "Narrow your list by dozens of criteria at once" / "Combine filters across academics, cost, location, size, and campus culture"
- All filters applied server-side as parameterized SQL for fast, reliable queries

**Filter Types Supported**:
- Full-text search (natural language matching)
- Substring matching
- Single and multi-select enums
- Numeric ranges with hard min/max bounds
- Exact string/numeric matches
- Geographic proximity with radius and bounding-box options
- Ranking filters (numeric ranges on rank positions)

---

### **2. RESULT VIEWING & EXPORT**

**Four Result Views**:
1. **Card view** (default) — responsive grid with summary stats
2. **List view** — compact row-based format for quick scanning
3. **Table view** — full data table with sortable columns, column visibility toggle, drag-to-reorder
4. **Map view** — interactive Leaflet map showing institution locations

**Result Actions**:
- "Export the shortlist as a PDF" / "Export a filtered list as CSV"
- CSV export includes all exportable fields and respects active filters, sort order, and user location
- PDF export includes data source attribution
- Both export types respect pagination, filtering, and sorting state
- View mode preference persists across sessions (localStorage)

**Sorting**:
- Sort any output field marked as sortable (ascending/descending)
- Sort by distance when user location is set
- When ranking blend is active, results automatically sorted by blend score
- Configurable page sizes: 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 results per page (max 100)
- All sort/pagination state encoded in shareable URLs

---

### **3. INSTITUTION DETAIL & COMPARISON**

**Detail View** (expandable drawer/modal):
- Core identity: name, city, state, coordinates
- Institution type and enrollment
- Admission and graduation rates
- Tuition (in-state and out-of-state) and average net price
- Retention rate and student-faculty ratio

**Similar Institutions**: "Find schools that truly fit"
- Query returns other schools with similar profiles based on:
  - Institution type, enrollment, admission rate, graduation rate, tuition
  - Similarity ranked by Euclidean distance score
  - Configurable limit (default 10, max 50)

**Column Customization** (Table View):
- Show/hide any output field via column picker
- Reorder columns via drag-and-drop or up/down controls
- Column visibility and order persist in localStorage
- Default-visible columns determined by field metadata

---

### **4. CUSTOM RANKING BLENDS**

- "Create your own custom ranking blend weighting cost and graduation rate to find the best value schools by your own criteria"
- Create weighted composite rankings from any combination of 7 sources:
  - US News National, US News Liberal Arts, Forbes, Washington Monthly (national & liberal arts), Niche, Money Magazine score
- Assign custom weights to each provider
- Results automatically sorted by computed blend score
- Blend weights serialized in URL for shareability
- NULL/unranked institutions penalized (normalize to worst score)

---

### **5. GEOGRAPHIC & LOCATION FEATURES**

**Proximity Search**:
- Filter by distance from any lat/lng coordinate (PostGIS ST_DWithin)
- Configurable radius: 0 to 500 km (~310 miles)
- "See how far each school is from your home"
- Distance calculated in miles per result

**User Location Services**:
- Set personal home location (latitude, longitude, state)
- Automatically used for distance calculations on every visit
- "My Price": automatic in-state tuition calculation for public schools in home state; out-of-state tuition otherwise
- ZIP code resolution: resolve any US ZIP code to latitude/longitude and state
- Location preferences persist for authenticated users

**Map View**:
- Interactive Leaflet map with markers for all results on current page
- Click-to-detail on markers

---

### **6. SAMPLE SEARCHES**

Home page features a carousel of pre-built searches demonstrating capabilities:
1. **Top 25 National** — US News National rank ≤ 25
2. **Affordable in California** — State = CA, net price ≤ $20,000
3. **D1 Small Schools** — NCAA Division I, enrollment ≤ 5,000
4. **Selective & Affordable** — Admission rate ≤ 30%, net price ≤ $20,000
5. **D3 New England** — NCAA Division III in New England region
6. **Sunny & Affordable** — Dry climate (Köppen B), net price ≤ $15,000

Each shows a live preview of matching institutions.

---

### **7. AUTHENTICATION & USER PREFERENCES**

**Sign-In Options**:
- OAuth-based authentication via Supabase
- Supported providers: Google, GitHub, email/password

**User Profile**:
- Query `me`: returns authenticated user's ID, email, display name, avatar URL, auth provider

**User Preferences** (authenticated users only):
- Save home location (latitude, longitude, state)
- Automatic provisioning: personal team created on first login
- Query `preferences`: retrieve saved preferences
- Mutation `updatePreferences`: update/replace preferences

**Automatic Features for Authenticated Users**:
- Distance calculations automatically use saved home location
- "My Price" automatically applies home state to tuition calculations
- View mode (card/list/table/map) preference persists across sessions
- Column layout and visibility persist across sessions
- "Returning users can save home location so distance and My Price are calculated automatically on every visit"

---

### **8. DATA AGGREGATION & SOURCES**

**Core Value Proposition**:
- "Consolidates data that is otherwise scattered across dozens of websites"
- "Aggregate data from the National Center for Education Statistics (NCES), major ranking providers, and other public sources"
- "Access detailed information on thousands of colleges — from acceptance rates and SAT scores to net price and graduation outcomes"
- "We bring together data from government surveys, major ranking providers, and other public sources"

**12 Integrated Data Sources**:
1. NCES (IPEDS) — federal survey data
2. US News (National Universities ranking)
3. US News (Liberal Arts Colleges ranking)
4. Forbes America's Top Colleges
5. Washington Monthly (national & liberal arts rankings)
6. Niche Best Colleges
7. Money Magazine Best Colleges
8. NCAA — division and conference data
9. FiveThirtyEight — partisan lean (congressional district)
10. Köppen Climate Classification
11. College Application Platforms (Common App, Coalition, Universal)
12. U.S. Census Bureau — metro area, locale classification

**Attribution**:
- Every search field linked to originating data source
- Data source badges and tooltips in UI
- `dataSources` GraphQL query returns full catalog with names, short names, URLs, descriptions
- Data source attribution included in PDF exports

---

### **9. AI & PROGRAMMATIC ACCESS**

**MCP (Model Context Protocol) Endpoint**:
- AI assistants can query the college database programmatically
- Two tools registered:
  1. `search_colleges` — execute filtered search with field/value pairs, limit, offset
  2. `get_search_fields` — retrieve full field descriptor catalog (types, options)
- WebMCP interface for OAuth 2.1 compliance
- Mandatory authentication (401 with WWW-Authenticate header if missing)

---

### **10. SHAREABILITY & TRANSPARENCY**

- All search parameters encoded in URL for easy sharing
- Blended rankings shareable via URL
- View mode can be set via URL parameter (`?view=table`)
- Pre-filtered searches can be shared with students/families
- "Share a search via URL to send a pre-filtered view to a student or family"
- "Sample searches as starting points for common student profiles"
- Mission statement emphasizes: "every student deserves free, open access to the full landscape of higher education data"
- No data behind paywalls

---

### **11. "WHY COLLEGE POWER SEARCH?" (HOME PAGE FEATURES)**

**3 Key Differentiators**:

1. **Comprehensive Data**
   - "Access detailed information on thousands of colleges — from acceptance rates and SAT scores to net price and graduation outcomes"

2. **Faceted Search**
   - "Narrow your list by dozens of criteria at once. Combine filters across academics, cost, location, size, and campus culture"

3. **Save & Compare**
   - "Build your personal college list, save searches, and compare schools side by side to make confident decisions"
   - *NOTE: "Save searches" and "personal college list" features referenced but not yet implemented in API; these are aspirational promises*

---

### **12. WHAT SETS KAPOW APART (ABOUT PAGE)**

**4 Highlighted Differentiators**:

1. **Comprehensive Data** — Aggregate data from NCES, major ranking providers, and other public sources
2. **Powerful Filters** — Search across dozens of dimensions (academics, admissions, cost, campus life, athletics, location, etc.) all at once
3. **Rankings & Comparisons** — View rankings from multiple providers side by side; build custom ranking blends
4. **Location-Aware** — Filter by proximity, see in-state vs. out-of-state costs, factor in climate preferences

**Mission Statement**:
"College Power Search exists to change that. We believe every student deserves free, open access to the full landscape of higher education data — and the tools to explore it on their own terms. Whether you care most about graduation rates, net price, campus safety, athletic programs, or research opportunities, you can build a search that reflects your priorities."

---

### **TARGET USERS**

- Prospective students researching colleges
- Parents and families comparing costs and outcomes
- High school counselors building filtered shortlists
- AI assistants querying college data programmatically via MCP

---

### **CRITICAL GAPS / ASPIRATIONAL PROMISES**

The following promises appear in the UI but are **NOT YET IMPLEMENTED** in the API:

1. **"Save & Compare"** / **"personal college list"** — Referenced on home page but no GraphQL mutations or storage for saved searches or college bookmarks
2. **"Campus safety"** — Mentioned in About page mission but no campus safety search field in the 48-field registry
3. **Saved searches** — Mentioned in user stories but no GraphQL mutation to save search configurations

These are likely future roadmap items (see `kapow-5oh: Saved search MVP` and `kapow-u9s: College bookmarks MVP` in the task list).

---

This summary captures every explicit user-facing promise made across the product documentation, home page, about page, and API schema.
